result:
Tonight, the city feels like a wet match-book. Everything flickers—neon, streetlamps, the blue-white glow behind apartment blinds—yet nothing seems willing to fully catch. Water beads on the windows in tiny crystal balls, reminding me of those childhood globes you shake to summon snow. Except this is all wrong-way weather for January, an apology that isn't making anyone less cold.

I keep thinking about the woman on the subway who looked at me as if she already knew the sentence I'd never write down: “I miss the version of you that existed before you needed reasons.” Her eyes, ringed by fatigue-indigo, reminded me that most of us carry secret cities behind our faces. Some days we let tourists in; most days we don't. She got off at Borough Hall without a goodbye, earbuds tethering her to a song whose title I'll never learn. Still, I keep hearing the fugitive echo of its bass line in the hollow of my ribs.

I’m eating oranges the way other people chain-smoke—peel, segment, bright burst of citrus oil that stings my chapped lips. A doctor once told me the oils murder bacteria on contact, said it like I wanted to disinfect myself from the inside out. Maybe I do. There’s something purifying about the small violence of tearing skin from flesh, sun caught under fingernails long after last light.

Over on Court Street, two men are arguing beneath a blurred awning about the maximum theoretical speed of regret. One says it moves faster than neutrinos, the other claims it’s stuck-weighted, a glacier that just looks still. Their hands carve and parry, conducting stray raindrops into shapes of punctuation. I want to tell them that regret has no velocity because it’s not traveling anywhere; it’s already here, co-inhabiting the body like a fog, a bit of old weather that won’t be moved by reason.

Half a block away, a bodega cat sprawls atop a crate of ginger beer, eyes slits of liquid moon. His nametag reads “Chairman Meow,” misspelled in glitter marker. He’s seen it all: the midnight transaction of black umbrellas, the woman who buys lilies every Thursday and leaves them at the river like currency for ghosts, the man who counts twenties with the dread of someone tallying minutes left to live. And still he purrs. Maybe the secret is knowing none of it is for us in the first place.

Back upstairs in Apartment 4R, I start deleting emails from the future. Spam bots keep sending me tomorrow's weather written in the past tense: “it rained and your hands were cold.” Who codes these messages, and why do they never include a subject line I can bear to open? I hover over “unsubscribe,” but pause at the thought that inside one of those sentences might be the one forewarning I actually need, the brief premonition that whispers *bridge earlier, kiss slower, trust the dark a little while longer.*

Instead I open a blank document, give it a filename that is just a date and an ampersand. Sometimes the only way forward is sideways, a crab shuffle across sand that remembers the shape of every foot that never returns. I write until the cursor stops blinking, exhausted, and for a moment the screen is a black pond reflecting a version of my face that looks as though it’s finally getting enough sleep.

Somewhere, outside this room, the city keeps pretending to be a city. Sirens braid with laughter, and the bridge lights necklace the river like low-hung stars. I feel all of it happening one layer above my skin, like a second sky stitched just millimeters away from the first. Maybe the difference between loneliness and solitude is simply the width of that seam.

And maybe tomorrow I’ll step outside at dawn, pockets full of unspent orange peels, and find the world has decided to combust after all, a bright and final flare against the reluctant night. I’ll raise some accidental toast to the woman on the subway, to Chairman Meow, to the arguing meteorologists of remorse. Until then, I keep the window cracked so the wet smoke of the city can roll in, tasting of batteries, sea salt, and the last few secrets that belong to everyone at once.
